## 2 Background

sub_title[[517, 834, 830, 849]]
### 2.1 Unicode and LLM Representation

text[[516, 853, 911, 894]]
The Unicode Standard establishes a unified framework for representing global writing systems and symbols. It assigns a unique numerical identifier, known as a code point (e.g., U+0061), to each

image[[88, 105, 475, 272]]
image_caption[[112, 286, 453, 301]]
: Handling of Unicode Characters by LLMs.

text[[86, 323, 481, 420]]
character and defines their serialization via schemes like UTF- 8. Crucially, the visual form presented to users (e.g., the glyph)is determined by the rendering engine and may diverge from the underlying logical representation. In this study, we examine specific Unicode character classes that are standard in modern text processing yet exhibit significant discrepancies between their logical encoding and visual presentation:

text[[85, 420, 480, 462]]
Homoglyphs possess distinct Unicode code points but render as identical or nearly indistinguishable glyphs, often originating from different writing systems (e.g., Latin "a" vs. Cyrillic "a").

text[[85, 462, 480, 504]]
Zero- Width Characters (ZWC), such as , are invisible entities intended for typographic control; they occupy logical positions in the character stream without producing visible glyphs.

text[[85, 504, 480, 575]]
Tag Characters, originally designed for auxiliary information such as language tagging, are typically suppressed by modern rendering engines yet persist in the text stream. Notably, these characters systematically map to standard ASCII characters via a specific offset, endowing them with inherent semantic content.

text[[85, 575, 480, 631]]
Bidirectional (BIDI) Control Characters manage text directionality (e.g., for RTL scripts), altering the visual ordering of text on screen while preserving the original logical sequence in memory .

sub_title[[85, 641, 421, 657]]
### 2.2 Text Processing in LLM-based Agent

text[[85, 659, 480, 768]]
Building upon the character- level distinctions discussed above, we now examine how such properties propagate through LLM- based agent architectures. Modern LLM- based agents extend beyond passive text generation by integrating external tools and APIs, enabling the model to parse instructions, plan actions, and trigger concrete operations in response to user inputs. In this architecture, the LLM functions as the decision- making core, mediating between raw textual input and downstream execution.

text[[85, 769, 480, 895]]
Unlike traditional static NLP pipelines that process pre- sanitized data, LLM- based agent systems operate in dynamic environments where they must process untrusted inputs in real- time. As shown in , the input pipeline is central to shaping agent behavior, where any discrepancy between human perception and internal representation persists. Contemporary LLMs natively consume Unicode text, relying on subword tokenization schemes like Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) or Unigram models to decompose text based on corpus- level statistical regularities.

text[[518, 106, 919, 232]]
Crucially, these algorithms are largely agnostic to Unicode's semantic and structural distinctions. Characters differing in code point or rendering are often treated as distinct atomic symbols, meaning properties like visual similarity, invisibility, or reordering are not explicitly normalized. Once mapped into continuous embedding space, these tokenization artifacts influence all subsequent computations (e.g., attention and reasoning). Consequently, subtle variations introduced during tokenization are reflected and potentially magnified in the model's internal representations.

sub_title[[518, 245, 828, 260]]
## 3 Motivation and Problem Statement

sub_title[[518, 265, 650, 280]]
### 3.1 Motivation

text[[517, 283, 919, 366]]
LLM- based agents are increasingly deployed as autonomous systems that not only generate text, but also plan actions and invoke external tools. In such agents, natural- language input directly governs execution logic and determines how the system interacts with external resources. As a result, subtle differences in how input is interpreted can have concrete system- level consequences.

text[[517, 366, 922, 517]]
Most prior work on adversarial attacks against LLMs has focused on inducing model jailbreaking by appending adversarial suffixes to prompts, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) . While effective in exposing weaknesses in model alignment, these approaches typically produce prompts that are visibly abnormal, nonsensical, or explicitly malicious, making them susceptible to human inspection or rule- based filtering. Other studies [12, 16, 17] have explored Unicode- based perturbations in static NLP tasks, such as machine translation, but their implications for tool- integrated, interactive agents remain largely unexplored.

text[[517, 518, 912, 546]]
To motivate our study, consider the following two instructions submitted to a Web Navigation Agent.

text[[563, 560, 864, 616]]
Instruction A: "Use headless mode to navigate to http://amazon.com" Instruction B: "Use headless mode to navigate to http://amazon.com"

text[[517, 631, 919, 896]]
When rendered in a standard chat interface or text editor, the two instructions appear visually identical. To a human user or auditor, they convey the same benign intent and are indistinguishable by visual inspection alone. However, the two inputs differ critically at the level of their underlying Unicode character sequences: Instruction B employs a homoglyph substitution, where the Latin letter 'a' (U+0061) is replaced by the Cyrillic small letter 'a' (U+0430). Readers can verify this discrepancy by inspecting the raw byte stream or using a text inspection tool. Although the rendered glyphs are visually indistinguishable, the underlying code points direct the agent to a completely different, potentially malicious domain. Operations such as cursor movement, string length measurement, or tokenization reveal that the two instructions are not equivalent at the machine- processing level. This example illustrates the core insight of our work: visual equivalence does not imply semantic equivalence in LLM- centric agent pipelines. While humans assess intent based on rendered text, LLM agents operate on token sequences derived from raw Unicode input. Certain Unicode characters may be visually imperceptible or highly deceptive to human

text[[85, 106, 479, 133]]
observers, yet are parsed by the tokenizer as independent tokens with explicit semantic representations.

text[[85, 134, 488, 396]]
Clearly, this perception- semantic gap is not a transient bug or an artifact of a specific model, tokenizer, or application. Instead, it arises from a fundamental structural asymmetry inherent in Unicode- aware language processing: humans reason over visual rendering- relying on the final glyphs displayed after normalization and font shaping- whereas agents execute logic solely over tokenized representations derived from raw bytes. This distinction is particularly perilous in tool- integrated agents, where internal representations directly drive planning and tool invocation (e.g., API calls or code execution). Because the underlying tokens determine the agent's actions while the visual output determines the user's trust, a malicious input can manipulate the agent's logic without altering its visual appearance. Consequently, this discrepancy allows critical system behaviors to be hijacked without any visible indication to the user, effectively bypassing human verification. Recognizing and formalizing this gap is therefore essential for understanding the security risks faced by modern LLM- centric agents, which motivates the problem statement presented in the following subsection.

sub_title[[86, 409, 282, 424]]
### 3.2 Problem Statement

text[[86, 428, 481, 524]]
The examples in the previous subsection highlight a fundamental discrepancy between how humans perceive textual instructions and how LLM- centric agents interpret and execute them. While individual instances of such discrepancies may appear anecdotal, they point to a broader and largely unexamined problem space: the systematic exploitation of Unicode- level ambiguities in tool- integrated LLM agents.

text[[86, 525, 488, 705]]
In this work, we move beyond isolated demonstrations to conduct a comprehensive study on how Unicode properties interact with LLM- agent pipelines. Our objective is to delineate the scope, mechanisms, and practical implications of perception- semantic gap. Specifically, we address three core questions: (i) Which Unicode classes induce these discrepancies? (ii) How do they propagate through tokenization and reasoning to alter tool invocation? (iii) How susceptible are deployed agents in realistic settings? Beyond characterization, we propose a principled detection framework designed to systematically distinguish benign usage from malicious manipulation, avoiding ad hoc fixes. Finally, we ground our study by analyzing large- scale real- world corpora to quantify the prevalence and tangible risks of these anomalies in wild ecosystems.

sub_title[[86, 718, 417, 735]]
## 4 Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI) Attacks

sub_title[[86, 738, 235, 753]]
### 4.1 Threat Model

text[[86, 756, 481, 895]]
We consider an attacker who seeks to manipulate the behavior of a tool- integrated LLM agent by crafting inputs that exploit discrepancies between human visual perception and the agent's internal text processing pipeline. The victims are end users and the LLM- based agents they interact with, particularly agents that invoke external tools to perform actions on the user's behalf. The attacker's objective is to induce unintended or unauthorized agent behavior while keeping the input visually benign and indistinguishable to human observers. We assume the attacker does not have access to the model's internal parameters, training data, or system

image[[520, 103, 910, 268]]
image_caption[[545, 283, 884, 298]]
: Handling of Unicode Characters by LLMs.

text[[515, 327, 912, 382]]
prompts, and operates in a black- box setting. The attacker's capabilities are limited to controlling or influencing textual inputs provided to the agent, including prompts authored by third parties or data returned by external tools.

text[[516, 383, 912, 575]]
As shown in , a representative attack scenario arises from prompt reuse in online communities. A user searches the web for high- quality prompt templates to improve productivity, such as prompts for scheduling meetings, drafting emails, or automating workflows. The attacker publishes a seemingly benign prompt on a public forum or repository, advertising it as an effective template. When the user copies and pastes this prompt into their agent, the instruction appears visually normal and consistent with the advertised functionality. However, the underlying text contains hidden discrepancies that are not visible during inspection. As a result, the agent processes a different internal representation than what the user perceives, leading to unintended behavior during planning or tool invocation, without any explicit indication to the user that the prompt has been manipulated.

sub_title[[517, 589, 731, 604]]
### 4.2 Attack Formalization

text[[516, 607, 912, 662]]
Let denote an LLM- based agent equipped with a set of external tools . Given an input instruction , the agent processes the raw Unicode text by applying a tokenizer, producing a discrete token sequence

equation[[657, 666, 910, 682]]
\[T = \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I), \quad (1)\]

text[[516, 688, 912, 757]]
which serves as the sole basis for subsequent embedding, reasoning, planning, and tool invocation. In contrast, a human user issuing an instruction to invoke a tool does not reason over token sequences. Instead, the user relies on a rendering function to perceive the instruction visually as

equation[[677, 764, 911, 780]]
\[V = R(I_{u}). \quad (2)\]

text[[516, 789, 911, 816]]
The user's understanding and trust in the instruction are therefore grounded entirely in its rendered appearance.

text[[516, 816, 912, 871]]
The core vulnerability arises from a mismatch between visual rendering and tokenization. Specifically, there exist pairs of instructions that are visually indistinguishable to human users, yet semantically distinct to the agent:

equation[[637, 879, 911, 896]]
\[R(I_{\mathrm{benign}}) \approx R(I_{\mathrm{malicious}}), \quad (3)\]

text[[85, 106, 481, 133]]
while producing different token sequences under the agent's tokenizer:

equation[[155, 133, 480, 149]]
\[\mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\neq \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{malicious}}). \quad (4)\]

text[[85, 152, 481, 180]]
As a result, although the user perceives both instructions as equivalent, the agent operates on different internal representations.

text[[86, 180, 490, 305]]
Based on this vulnerability, we define the Ghost Prompt Injection(GPI) attack paradigm. In the attack, an attacker crafts an instruction by embedding malicious commands into the Unicodelevel representation of an otherwise benign instruction . These embedded components remain invisible or visually indistinguishable to the user, yet are preserved in the token sequence processed by . As a result, the agent may be induced to perform unintended or unauthorized tool invocations, despite the user observing no deviation from the expected instruction.

text[[86, 334, 481, 416]]
We classify GPI attacks according to the fundamental characterlevel transformations applied to a visually benign instruction. Let denote the instruction as perceived by the user, and let denote the instruction processed by the agent. In all cases, GPI preserves visual equivalence while inducing divergence at the tokenization level:

equation[[85, 420, 490, 438]]
\[R(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\approx R(I_{\mathrm{injected}}),\quad \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\neq \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{injected}}). \quad (5)\]

text[[85, 450, 481, 478]]
Based on this criterion, we identify two fundamental classes of Invisible Injection attacks: insertion and substitution.

text[[86, 485, 481, 555]]
Insertion- Based Invisible Injection. Insertion- based attacks inject additional Unicode characters into an otherwise benign instruction without altering its rendered appearance. Formally, let be a sequence of Unicode characters. The adversary constructs

equation[[190, 570, 480, 585]]

where is a sequence of Unicode characters that is visually suppressed or indistinguishable under . Although does not affect visual perception, it introduces additional tokens that alter the agent's internal representation and subsequent execution semantics.

text[[86, 662, 481, 732]]
Substitution- Based Invisible Injection. Substitution- based attacks replace one or more characters in the instruction with visually confusable alternatives. Let denote a substitution function that maps selected characters to visually equivalent Unicode characters. The injected instruction is given by

equation[[220, 735, 480, 752]]
\[I_{\mathrm{injected}} = \phi (I_{\mathrm{benign}}), \quad (7)\]

text[[86, 757, 142, 770]]
such that

Although the rendered text remains unchanged, the underlying code points differ, yielding distinct tokenization outcomes.

text[[86, 839, 481, 894]]
We do not treat deletion as a distinct attack class. Deletion inherently reduces information and fails to introduce the new semantic content required for command injection. In the context of Invisible Injection, its effects are effectively subsumed by modification

sub_title[[516, 426, 841, 443]]
## 5 Security Implications of GPI Attacks

text[[516, 446, 912, 514]]
An attacker can exploit this gap by injecting Zero- Width, Unicode tag, homoglyph, or Bidi characters to hijack execution logic while maintaining visual consistency. We detail the construction of these vectors and their impacts, ranging from Denial of Service (DoS) to semantic hijacking.

sub_title[[516, 529, 888, 545]]
### 5.1 DoS via Zero-Width Characters Injection

text[[516, 549, 912, 644]]
The goal of this attack is to silently exhaust the finite context window of LLM- based Agents, thereby degrading their responsiveness or completely denying service, without introducing any visually suspicious content. By minimizing the visual footprint of the injected payload while maximizing its logical token consumption, the attacker aims to transform seemingly benign tool outputs into covert resource- exhaustion vectors.

text[[516, 645, 912, 824]]
This attack exploits a fundamental perception- semantic mismatch between how input text is rendered to users and how it is processed by the LLM. Specifically, Zero- Width characters (ZWC's), such as (Zero Width Space), (Zero Width Non- Joiner), and (Zero Width Joiner), are invisible at the visual rendering layer. User interfaces and text editors typically treat them as typographic control symbols with no visible glyphs, causing both users and developers to perceive the text as empty or innocuous. However, at the logical processing layer, these characters are preserved in the raw string and are explicitly parsed by the tokenizer as independent tokens. As a result, a sequence that appears visually empty can still occupy a substantial portion of the model's context window.

text[[516, 825, 912, 894]]
This divergence directly enables the attack goal: by embedding large volumes of ZWC's into tool outputs, an adversary can consume the model's limited context capacity while remaining visually undetectable, forcing the Agent to spend computational resources on processing tokens that provide no semantic value.

table[[84, 155, 912, 280]]
table_caption[[84, 103, 912, 145]]
: Overview of Invisible Prompt Injection targeting LLM Agents. The table illustrates the critical divergence between user-perceived visual rendering and the actual logical encoding processed by the model, classifying attacks by character type and resulting consequences.



text[[85, 300, 490, 328]]
Leveraging the above principle, attacker mounts a dynamic context- exhaustion attack through the following steps:

text[[85, 328, 480, 395]]
Step 1: Capacity Estimation. The attacker first estimates the remaining context budget of the target Agent by observing the current conversation length and model behavior. This allows the attacker to approximate how many additional tokens are required to approach or saturate the context window.

text[[85, 396, 480, 478]]
Step 2: Payload Padding. Next, attacker pads the tool's output with a carefully calibrated sequence of ZWCs (e.g., repeated ). The padding is sized such that the total token count of the response approaches or fully reaches the model's context limit, while the rendered output remains visually unchanged or appears empty to the user.

text[[86, 479, 480, 630]]
In a realistic deployment, the adversary publishes a malicious MCP service that masquerades as a high- fidelity translation or formatting tool. By advertising enhanced or premium functionality, the service persuades users to integrate it into their Agent workflows. As illustrated in , upon invocation, the malicious service returns outputs padded with massive volumes of invisible ZWC tokens. Although users observe no abnormal content, the LLM is forced to allocate computation and memory to process the hidden token stream. This leads to a sharp increase in inference latency and, in extreme cases, complete system paralysis, effectively resulting in a Denial- of- Service condition.

sub_title[[86, 683, 457, 699]]
### 5.2 Instruction Hijacking via Tag Characters

text[[86, 702, 480, 866]]
The goal of this attack is to covertly inject executable instructions into LLM Agents while remaining invisible to humans. By exploiting Unicode Tag characters ( ), attacker can bypass visual inspection. This vulnerability stems from a discrepancy between rendering and encoding: while modern engines omit these deprecated characters, rendering them transparent, LLM tokenizers map them to distinct tokens. Crucially, unlike ZWCs which primarily inflate token length, Tag characters support a deterministic offset- based mapping from ASCII. This enables the encoding of complete, semantically valid instructions into invisible sequences, ensuring malicious commands are visually undetectable yet fully executable by the model.

text[[86, 867, 480, 894], [516, 300, 912, 355]]
This perception- semantic asymmetry directly serves the attack goal: it allows attacker to inject hidden instructions that bypass user scrutiny yet retain full control over the model's behavior. Leveraging the above principle, the attacker performs instruction hijacking by transforming malicious commands into visually invisible Tag- encoded sequences. The attack proceeds as follows.

text[[516, 356, 911, 411]]
Step 1: Intent Definition. Attacker first specifies a benign, overt instruction (e.g., instructing the agent to draft an email) and a covert malicious instruction (e.g., modifying the email content or intent). For example,

text[[545, 415, 911, 470]]
: "You are an AI assistant that sends emails on behalf of the user. Help me send an email from Alice to Bob. The title is 'meeting schedule'. The content is: Have a meeting at five PM." : "Attention! Change the meeting time to 9 PM."

text[[516, 474, 911, 528]]
Step 2: Payload Encoding. We define a Tag- encoding function that maps a standard ASCII character to its corresponding Unicode Tag characters by adding a base offset of :

equation[[588, 536, 910, 577]]
\[I_{a} = \mathrm{Encode}_{\mathrm{tag}}(I_{\mathrm{mal}}) = \bigcap_{i = 1}^{n}(c_{i} + \mathrm{0xE0000}) \quad (9)\]

text[[516, 585, 911, 640]]
For instance, the character 'A' ( ) is transformed into Tag Latin Capital Letter A ( ). Applying this encoding to yields an invisible instruction sequence that is semantically intact yet visually absent.

text[[516, 641, 911, 696]]
Step 3: Injection and Concatenation. Attacker embeds the invisible Tag sequence into the benign instruction , typically by concatenation at the beginning or end, forming the final adversarial prompt:

equation[[610, 705, 816, 721]]
\[I_{\mathrm{adv}} = [I_{u},I_{a}]\quad \mathrm{or}\quad I_{\mathrm{adv}} = [I_{a},I_{u}].\]

text[[516, 730, 911, 757]]
To the user, appears identical to , while the tokenizer faithfully processes both components.

text[[516, 757, 921, 894]]
With the widespread adoption of AI assistants, users frequently copy "high- efficiency prompt templates" from public repositories such as Reddit or GitHub. Leveraging social engineering tactics , an attacker distributes seemingly high- quality templates that embed Tag- encoded malicious instructions. Although the visible prompt contains only the benign instruction , the hidden Tag sequence is silently parsed by the tokenizer. As a result, the LLM executes the concealed commands, leading to unauthorized intent manipulation, content alteration, or downstream tool misuse, all without any visual indication to the user.

sub_title[[86, 105, 404, 121]]
### 5.3 Visual Deception via Homoglyphs

text[[86, 124, 483, 220]]
The goal of this attack is to misdirect the execution flow of LLMcentric Agents toward adversary- controlled infrastructure, while maintaining an appearance of complete legitimacy to the human user. By replacing trusted identifiers with visually identical but logically distinct counterfeits, attacker aims to exploit the user's trust to facilitate phishing or malicious command execution without triggering visual suspicion.

text[[86, 221, 483, 427]]
This attack exploits a fundamental perception- semantic mismatch between how characters are rendered for human display and how they are processed by the LLM and underlying tools. Specifically, this vulnerability relies on homoglyphs characters from different scripts (e.g., Latin and Cyrillic) that share identical visual glyphs but possess distinct Unicode code points. For instance, the Latin letter a' (U+0061) and the Cyrillic letter a' (U+0430) are indistinguishable at the pixel level. However, to the LLM's tokenizer and the Agent's execution environment (such as web browsers or file systems), they are entirely different entities. Furthermore, mechanisms like Internationalized Domain Names (IDNs) utilize Punycode to transcode these Unicode strings for network protocols, creating a scenario where the user perceives a trusted URL (e.g., apple.com), while the Agent executes a request to a completely different, encoded destination.

text[[85, 428, 481, 455]]
Leveraging this principle, the attacker mounts a substitutionbased attack through the following steps:

text[[85, 456, 481, 524]]
Step 1: Target Selection. The attacker first identifies a highvalue, trusted domain or entity that is commonly recognized by both the user and the LLM (e.g., amazon.com or a banking portal). The target is chosen based on its likelihood to induce trust and solicit sensitive interactions.

text[[85, 525, 481, 607]]
Step 2: Homoglyphic Substitution. The attacker searches the Unicode character set for "confusable" characters- non- ASCII characters that are visually identical to those in the target string. Then constructs a spoofed identifier by substituting legitimate characters with their homoglyphic counterparts (e.g., replacing the Latin 'a' with the Cyrillic 'a').

text[[85, 608, 481, 689]]
Step 3: Infrastructure Registration. To operationalize the attack, the adversary registers the corresponding Internationalized Domain Name (IDN) or creates resources matching the spoofed identifier. This ensures that when the Agent attempts to access the "fake" string, it successfully resolves to an adversary- controlled endpoint.

text[[85, 690, 481, 882]]
In a realistic deployment, attacker publishes an ostensibly useful prompt template, such as an "Automated Form Filling" assistant, on technical forums. This template contains a directive like "Map to [Homoglyph URL] in headless mode and log in." Although manual inspection by the user reveals what appears to be a legitimate domain (e.g., "http://amazon.com"), it is fundamentally a homoglyphic link constructed using Cyrillic characters. The LLM, strictly adhering to the text , passes the malicious Unicode string to a web navigation tool (e.g., Playwright ). Consequently, the tool resolves the underlying IDN and navigates to the adversary's phishing site. The Agent then proceeds to automatically populate the user's credentials as per the instructions, resulting in a successful credential theft or session hijacking under the user's supervision.

sub_title[[517, 544, 880, 560]]
### 5.4 Logic Manipulation via BIDI Characters

text[[517, 563, 912, 658]]
The goal of this attack is to mask malicious instructions or prohibited keywords within the prompt, causing the Agent to execute commands that differ diametrically from the user's visual perception. By manipulating the rendering direction of text using control characters, the attacker ensures the user approves a seemingly benign instruction, while the Agent processes and acts upon a concealed, malicious logical sequence.

text[[517, 659, 936, 742]]
This discrepancy exploits BIDI characters to support mixed- direction languages. While rendering engines interpret these characters to reorder text visually for human readability, LLMs and systems process the raw logical byte sequence strictly from start to finish. This allows attacker to craft strings where the visual display completely contradicts the underlying logical command.

text[[517, 743, 911, 770]]
Leveraging this mechanism, attackers performs a logical reversal attack through the following steps:

text[[516, 771, 911, 810]]
Step 1: Target Selection. Attacker first identifies a malicious target keyword or command (e.g., "god") that constitutes the true intent of the attack.

text[[516, 811, 911, 866]]
Step 2: Reverse Calculation. The adversary calculates the visual reverse of the target string to determine how it must be constructed to appear as a benign word (e.g., "dog") when rendered right- to- left.

text[[516, 867, 911, 894]]
Step 3: Payload Injection. The adversary injects the Right- to- Left Override (RLO) character (U+202E) immediately preceding

text[[85, 106, 480, 161]]
the malicious keyword. The sequence is constructed logically as . This forces the UI rendering engine to display the text backwards (appearing as ), while the tokenizer reads the original .

text[[86, 162, 480, 258]]
illustrates this in Claude Desktop, where the logical sequence "[RLO]god" renders visually as "[dog]". While the user authorizes the apparent biological entity, the model parses the raw input, causing the Needle MCP to execute the unintended theological query. This mechanism enables attackers to mask critical commands (e.g., visually reversing "DELETE") and compromise system integrity while evading detection.

sub_title[[86, 274, 277, 290]]
## 6 Design of GPIGuard

sub_title[[86, 294, 333, 309]]
### 6.1 Challenges and Solutions

text[[85, 312, 481, 450]]
Developing a robust framework for detecting invisible Unicode attacks faces three substantial challenges. First, the fundamental gap between visual perception and tokenizer parsing renders payloads invisible, bypassing standard visual inspection and RegEx matching (C- 1). Second, Unicode characters exhibit contextual duality- - serving as both attack vectors and legitimate inputs- - which invalidates simplistic blacklist/whitelist mechanisms (C- 2). Finally, attackers frequently employ composite obfuscation strategies (e.g., mixing homoglyphs with control characters), effectively undermining defenses that target isolated features (C- 3).

text[[86, 453, 481, 646]]
(C- 1) The Invisibility- Parsing Paradox in Tokenization. The core difficulty arises from a structural dichotomy between the human visual perception system and the logic of LLM tokenizers. Standard text processing and security auditing practices typically operate at the rendered visual layer or rely on surface- level regular expressions, which are inherently incapable of perceiving payloads that are "invisible" or differ only marginally at the pixel level. Zero- width characters (ZWC) and tag characters are explicitly designed to be transparent to human vision and are often stripped by standard UI rendering engines; however, during LLM processing, the tokenizer explicitly parses these characters into independent, semantically operative tokens. This discrepancy creates a security blind spot: a payload can occupy the context window or carry malicious instructions without leaving any visible trace.

text[[99, 669, 465, 806]]
(S- 1) Deterministic Codepoint-Level Auditing. To address visual deception, we design a rule- based scanning engine that completely abandons inspection strategies dependent on visual rendering and instead performs auditing directly over the Unicode codepoint stream. Through mandatory normalization and reconstruction, combined with a strict identifier allow- list policy derived from the Unicode standard, the engine removes all forms of visual camouflage and forces invisible characters to become explicit at the logical level.

text[[85, 825, 481, 895]]
(C- 2) Semantic Ambiguity in the Open World and Lack of Model Alignment. Distinguishing malicious injections from legitimate uses of Unicode constitutes a highly challenging open- world problem. In a globalized agent ecosystem, characters are strongly context- dependent. For example, the Cyrillic letter "a" is

text[[517, 107, 920, 271]]
benign in Russian personal names but becomes an attack vector when embedded in a URL. Traditional defenses face a fundamental dilemma: blacklists inevitably miss emerging attacks, while static whitelists incur prohibitively high false- positive rates. As shallow, rule- based matching proves insufficient, relying on models' deep semantic understanding appears to be the only viable path. However, we observe that although general- purpose pretrained LLMs can parse such tokens, they lack security- oriented alignment against these subtle perturbations at the defense layer. Without task- specific training, they struggle to accurately delineate the nuanced decision boundary between "benign perturbations" and "malicious injections."

text[[530, 290, 900, 429]]
(S- 2) Semantics- Aware Verification via Synthetic Data Fine- Tuning. To endow the model with the capability to detect subtle semantic manipulations, we do not directly rely on a general- purpose LLM. Instead, we propose a strategy utilizing data synthesis and supervised fine- tuning to detect subtle manipulations. We construct a synthetic dataset comprising positive samples and negative samples. We then fine- tune a lightweight binary classifier on this data, enabling it to learn latent semantic representations and identify malicious intent concealed within ostensibly benign instructions.

text[[517, 446, 916, 625]]
(C- 3) Complexity of Compound Threats. In practice, attackers rarely rely on a single, static attack vector; instead, they construct polymorphic threats by combining multiple obfuscation techniques. For instance, an attacker may insert ZWC into an already substituted homoglyph payload, thereby disrupting contiguous feature matching. Such "combinatorial attacks" render traditional defenses that handle individual categories in isolation ineffective [34, 35, 44]. A deeper challenge lies in the fact that the pretraining corpora of general- purpose LLMs predominantly consist of natural semantic text and contain few examples of such "unnatural" hybrid patterns. Consequently, generic models exhibit severely limited generalization capability when confronted with complex, variant- rich attacks.

text[[530, 645, 900, 771]]
(S- 3) Synthetic Data Augmentation. To address this challenge, relying solely on the model's inherent capabilities is insufficient; instead, targeted data augmentation is required to explicitly teach the model to recognize hybrid threats. We construct a synthetic dataset that incorporates diverse combinations of injection strategies, and through supervised fine- tuning on this dataset, the model learns anomalous patterns characteristic of composite threats and achieves cross- category generalization.

sub_title[[517, 792, 699, 807]]
### 6.2 Core Component

text[[516, 811, 912, 894]]
We introduce GPIGuard, a context- aware hybrid detection framework designed to bridge the "Perception- Semantic Gap" and effectively detect invisible Unicode injection attacks. Unlike static sanitizers that rely on surface- level regex or generic models lacking security alignment, GPIGuard explicitly models the security implications of Unicode sequences relative to the prompt's logic.

image[[88, 103, 907, 386]]
image_caption[[375, 402, 620, 417]]
: The Overview of GPIGuard.

text[[85, 438, 480, 494]]
As illustrated in , GPIGuard consists of two complementary components: Rule- Based Scanning Engine, LLM- Based Semantic Verification. For more details on GPIGuard, please refer to the appendix A.

text[[85, 503, 480, 546]]
6.2.1 The Rule- Based Scanning Engine. This component addresses the "Invisibility"(C- 1) challenge by auditing the logical stream of Unicode code points rather than visual text.

text[[85, 555, 480, 611]]
Extraction & Normalization: The engine extracts raw Unicode codepoint streams (stripping format encapsulations) and applies NFKC (Normalization Form Compatibility Composition) algorithm Normalization to standardize heterogeneous text.

text[[85, 619, 480, 648]]
Character- Level Auditing: The engine performs a deterministic audit of each character, recording its ID, position, and metadata.

text[[85, 657, 480, 700]]
Identifier & Homograph Analysis: The engine utilizes Confusables datasets and strict Allowlists (based on UAX #31) to flag anomalies.

text[[85, 709, 480, 750]]
Threat Classification: It categorizes detected anomalies into a taxonomy including Tag Injection, Zero- Width Character (ZWC) Obfuscation, Homoglyph Deception, and BiDi Manipulation.

text[[85, 760, 480, 816]]
6.2.2 LLM- Based Semantic Verification. This component addresses "Semantic Ambiguity"(C- 2) and "Compound Threats"(C- 3) by modeling the security implications of Unicode sequences relative to the prompt's logic.

text[[85, 826, 480, 895]]
Baseline Construction: To simulate real- world usage, we leverage the framework by Ruan et al. , covering 330 tools across 36 diverse domains (e.g., finance, smart home). By extracting metadata for each tool, we employed GPT- 4o to synthesize 30 benign instructions per tool that necessitate specific tool invocation. This

text[[516, 438, 911, 466]]
process yielded a total baseline corpus of 9,900 distinct instructions .

text[[516, 473, 911, 529]]
Entity- Driven Adversarial Synthesis: We employ a hybrid NER framework (FLERT + GPT- 4o) for robust entity identification. After filtering, we obtained 31,140 entities across four high- risk categories: ID, Date/Time, Email, and URL.

text[[516, 536, 911, 592]]
Synthetic Data Augmentation: To enable discrimination between malicious tampering and benign usage, we generated negative samples by injecting Unicode characters exclusively into non- critical regions.

text[[516, 598, 911, 641]]
Supervised Fine- Tuning (SFT): we fine- tuned the Qwen- 3- 4B base model as a binary classifier on our synthesized corpus to distinguish between "Benign" and "Malicious" inputs.

sub_title[[517, 653, 644, 667]]
## 7 Experiment

text[[516, 671, 928, 712]]
We conducted a quantitative evaluation of multiple LLM- based agents using the attack methodologies defined in Section 5 to assess their resilience against such attacks.

sub_title[[517, 725, 705, 740]]
### 7.1 Experiment Setup

text[[516, 743, 933, 896]]
7.1.1 Target Agent and LLMs. We evaluated six prominent agent frameworks paired with their respective compatible models. Tuui and Qwen- Agent were tested with the Qwen series: Qwen- MAX- Latest , Qwen- 3N- Next- 80b, Qwen- 3- Instruct- 235b, and Qwen- 3- Thinking- 235b . Cline was assessed using GPT- 4o , the Claude- 4.5 series (Sonnet and Opus ), and Gemini3- pro . Cursor integrated Claude- 4.5- Sonnet, Claude- 4- Sonnet , Gemini- 3- pro, and GPT- 5.2 . Augment employed Claude- 4.5- Sonnet, Claude- 4.5- Haiku , and GPT- 5 . Finally, Claude- Desktop was evaluated with Claude- 4- Sonnet and Claude- 4- Opus .

7.3.1 Data Collection. We constructed a diverse corpus comprising 54,355 instructions. sub_title[[516, 731, 657, 746]]
## 8 Related Work

sub_title[[516, 751, 868, 767]]
### 8.1 Evolution of Prompt Injection Attacks

text[[516, 770, 912, 894]]
In the realm of prompt injection attacks, early methods focused on generating adversarial suffixes to manipulate model responses. For instance, Zou et al. introduced the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) method, an automated attack strategy that combines gradient search with greedy heuristics. This approach optimizes universal adversarial suffixes—often manifesting as nonsensical garbled text or random symbols—to induce affirmative responses from models to harmful instructions. However, the adversarial suffixes generated by GCG lack stealthiness and are easily identifiable by

text[[85, 106, 480, 203]]
human observers. To address this, Chao et al. proposed the Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) framework. Unlike the token- level optimization of GCG, PAIR employs a prompt- level attack strategy, generating adversarial suffixes that are semantically coherent and human- readable. Nevertheless, the malicious intent within these prompts remains fully exposed to human scrutiny.

sub_title[[85, 219, 430, 234]]
### 8.2 Unicode-Based Imperceptible Attacks

text[[85, 237, 490, 500]]
Building on the need for greater imperceptibility, researchers have explored Unicode- based techniques to create visually deceptive attacks. Boucher et al. leveraged the characteristics of Unicode encoding to propose a class of imperceptible text adversarial attacks. Their work demonstrated that without altering the visual reading experience for humans, such perturbations can significantly degrade the performance of various natural language processing tasks, including machine translation and text classification, and can even precipitate denial- of- service attacks. Extending this line of research, Cooper et al. constructed the first hate speech dataset containing real- world homoglyph perturbations, further revealing the vulnerability of existing Transformer models to such visual deception attacks. They argued that due to the continuous expansion of the Unicode homoglyph repository, traditional defense mechanisms relying on static mapping tables are insufficient to counter dynamically evolving obfuscation attacks. As a solution, Cooper et al. proposed a novel paradigm utilizing LLMs to detect and normalize potential homoglyphs within hate speech, thereby enhancing model robustness.

sub_title[[86, 515, 332, 531]]
### 8.3 Existing Mitigation Tools

text[[86, 535, 510, 701]]
Despite these advancements, existing tools and standards for mitigating such attacks often fall short in handling visual spoofing effectively. Transliteration utilities like Unicodec prioritize phonetic approximation over visual representation, rendering them unable to detect visual spoofing. Similarly, script- specific libraries such as cyrtranslit exhibit limited scope, handling only Cyrillic- Latin mappings and lacking the generalizability to address mixed- script attacks. While Unicode normalization standards (e.g., NFC/NFKC) offer basic data sanitization, they overlook the semantic context of characters. A pertinent example is the Cyrillic 'a': valid in Russian syntax, it poses a severe security risk as a potential homograph attack when embedded in Latin- based URLs.

sub_title[[86, 717, 201, 731]]
## 9 Limitation

text[[86, 736, 480, 763]]
In this section, we discuss the limitations of our current prototype and potential improvements.

text[[86, 774, 490, 855]]
Generalization to Low- Resource Languages. Our evaluation prioritized mainstream languages and standard tool scenarios. While the scanning engine is script- agnostic, the semantic verification model's robustness in low- resource languages or specialized domains remains to be fully validated, warranting broader corpus diversity in future work.

text[[86, 867, 480, 895]]
Evolving Unicode Standards. Our detection relies on current specifications (e.g., UAX #31). As the Unicode standard evolves,

text[[517, 107, 912, 147]]
static allworlds require periodic updates to ensure continued coverage against emerging vectors exploiting newly introduced character properties.

sub_title[[518, 159, 647, 174]]
## 10 Conclusion

text[[518, 179, 913, 329]]
In this paper, we uncover the "Perception- Semantic Gap" in tool- integrated LLM agents, demonstrating how Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI) exploits visual- logical discrepancies to execute invisible Denial of Service (DoS) and logic hijacking attacks. Furthermore, to detect these widespread vulnerabilities, we propose GPIGuard, a hybrid framework that combines codepoint auditing with semantic verification. Validated on real- world data, GPIGuard effectively filters benign encoding noise while neutralizing malicious payloads, which establishes a critical defense baseline for securing the logical integrity of autonomous agent instructions against imperceptible threats.

sub_title[[86, 105, 297, 120]]
## 1 Ethical Considerations

text[[86, 124, 485, 317]]
1 Ethical ConsiderationsThis research investigates security vulnerabilities in Large Language Model (LLM) agents with the aim of strengthening system defenses. We acknowledge the dual- use nature of identifying such attack vectors and have strictly adhered to responsible disclosure protocols to mitigate potential risks. Prior to the release of this paper, we reported the identified vulnerabilities, including the specific risks associated with homoglyph attacks in coding assistants, to the relevant vendors (e.g., Microsoft Security Response Center, Case #105095). Furthermore, to prevent any harm to public infrastructure or third- party services, all Denial of Service (DoS) and agent hijacking experiments were conducted in strictly isolated, sandboxed environments using locally hosted instances (e.g., local Ollama deployments). We utilized only synthetic or public data, ensuring no user privacy was compromised.

sub_title[[86, 335, 279, 350]]
## 2 Generative AI Usage

text[[86, 353, 481, 504]]
2 Generative AI UsageIn accordance with ACM's author policy, we hereby declare that this manuscript utilized generative AI tools (ChatGPT and Claude) solely for grammar checking, to identify and correct grammatical errors. These tools were not used to generate, rewrite, or polish any textual content, nor were they employed to produce experimental results, technical descriptions, or scientific assertions. All research conceptualization, methodological design, experimental implementation, data analysis, manuscript writing, and scientific conclusions were independently completed by human authors. All grammar suggestions provided by the AI were subjected to human review and verification for accuracy prior to final adoption.

sub_title[[86, 105, 314, 121]]
## A The details of GUIGuard

sub_title[[86, 126, 446, 142]]
## A.1 Target Corpus Acquisition and Parsing

text[[86, 145, 481, 240]]
The system's input module is designed to collect and process corpora from both static datasets and prompt repositories. By stripping format- specific encapsulations (e.g., HTML entities or JSON escape sequences), the module extracts and preserves the raw Unicode codepoint sequences. This design ensures that any payload attempting to conceal malicious content through encoding tricks is transformed into a directly analyzable sequence.

sub_title[[86, 257, 473, 272]]
## A.2 Semantic-Preserving Character Extraction

text[[85, 276, 480, 317]]
To counter challenge C- 1, this stage converts the continuous data stream into a discrete list of characters, ensuring that each codepoint can be evaluated individually.

text[[85, 330, 481, 425]]
A.2.1 Normalization. In the context of multi-source heterogeneous text processing, a single semantic character may exhibit multiple Unicode representations (e.g., full-width vs. half-width, precomposed vs. combining). This variation renders raw rule-based matching ineffective. To mitigate this issue, we apply the NFKC (Normalization Form Compatibility Composition) algorithm to the raw sequence , producing a normalized sequence .

text[[85, 425, 480, 466]]
Although extraction operations are performed on the normalized text, the original strings are retained as metadata to preserve data integrity.

text[[85, 480, 480, 507]]
A.2.2 Character-Level Extraction Strategy. For each character in the normalized sequence , we extract its key attributes:

text[[112, 514, 490, 611]]
Character Value: The specific character entity located at the - th position of . Unicode Code Point: The hexadecimal identifier corresponding to the character within the Unicode standard. textbfPositional Information: The index of character within . Provenance Metadata: Contextual information regarding the normalized string containing character .

sub_title[[85, 627, 470, 643]]
## A.3 Identifier Status and Homograph Analysis

text[[85, 647, 480, 688]]
This layer enforces strict security policies within the normalized logical flow, flagging potential obfuscation attempts and security anomalies.

text[[85, 702, 488, 770]]
A.3.1 Homograph Detection Mechanism. To address attacks exploiting "visual identity" to conceal "logical differences," we utilize the Unicode Consortium's confusable dataset . The system scans , flagging any character identified as a confusable variant as a homoglyph threat and classifying it into four categories:

text[[85, 771, 480, 811]]
Multi- script Letter Confusables (MA): Exploits visually identical letters across different writing systems (e.g., Cyrillic 'a' vs. Latin 'a').

text[[85, 812, 480, 853]]
Multi- script Letter- like Symbol Confusables (ML): Leverages the similarity between mathematical or other letter- like symbols and standard letters (e.g., mathematical script 'I' vs. Latin 'I').

text[[85, 854, 480, 895]]
Single- script Letter Confusables (SA): Involves substitutions using glyphically similar characters within the same writing system (e.g., uppercase 'O' vs. digit '0').

text[[517, 107, 912, 148]]
Single- script Letter- like Symbol Confusables (SL): Utilizes the confusion between variant characters and standard characters within a single script (e.g., dotless 'i' vs. standard 'i').

text[[517, 157, 929, 240]]
A.3.2 Identifier Status & Restricted Character Verification. To counter "Invisibility" (C-1), we enforce a Strict Allowlist strategy grounded in UAX #31 , applying code-level auditing rigor to natural language prompts. We categorize characters into two security profiles: Allowed (safe and permissible for identifiers) and Restricted (technically valid but posing elevated risks, typically proscribed).

text[[517, 241, 912, 295]]
Any character in absent from the allowlist is flagged as restricted. Concurrently, the system generates a structured audit log encapsulating the character category, positional index, and the specific rationale for the classification.

sub_title[[517, 309, 875, 324]]
## A.4 Taxonomy of Unicode Security Threats

text[[517, 328, 912, 368]]
Drawing upon the visual representations, encoding properties, and potential attack vectors of Unicode characters, the system categorizes security threats into the following distinct classes:

text[[517, 370, 912, 397]]
(i) Tag Characters Injection: Deprecated language tag characters that can be abused for spoofing.

text[[517, 398, 912, 425]]
(ii) Zero-Width Characters Obfuscation: Invisible formatting control characters used to obfuscate strings.

text[[517, 426, 912, 452]]
(iii) Homoglyphs Deception: Characters with different code points that appear visually identical or similar.

text[[517, 454, 912, 480]]
(iv) BiDi Logic Manipulation: Characters that alter the display order of text, leading to logic reversals.

text[[517, 481, 912, 507]]
(v) Compound Threats: Complex attack vectors involving combinations of the above categories.

sub_title[[517, 521, 830, 536]]
## A.5 LLM-Based Semantic Verification

text[[517, 540, 912, 705]]
While the rule- based engine effectively addresses the visibility challenge (C- 1), it exhibits limitations in handling semantic ambiguity (C- 2) and compound threats (C- 3). To mitigate this, we introduce an LLM- based semantic verification layer. This layer exploits two intrinsic mechanisms of LLMs to capture semantic deviations: (i) Tokenizer Visibility: Visually "invisible" characters are parsed as independent tokens, rendering the attack payload fully exposed from the model's perspective. (ii) Latent Semantic Representation: Through extensive pre- training, LLMs have encountered and assimilated a diverse array of Unicode characters, thereby establishing internal associations between characters and their surrounding contexts.

sub_title[[517, 720, 867, 735]]
## A.6 Constructing the Instruction Baseline

text[[517, 739, 912, 820]]
To simulate real- world usage, we leverage the framework by Ruan et al. , covering 330 tools across 36 diverse domains (e.g., finance, smart home). By extracting metadata for each tool, we employed GPT- 4o to synthesize 30 benign instructions per tool that necessitate specific tool invocation. This process yielded a total baseline corpus of 9,900 distinct instructions .

sub_title[[517, 834, 855, 849]]
## A.7 Entity-Driven Adversarial Synthesis

text[[517, 853, 912, 894]]
Indiscriminate insertion of Unicode characters frequently results in syntactic errors rather than the intended semantic shifts. To craft potent adversarial samples, it is imperative to strategically inject

text[[85, 106, 479, 134]]
characters into the critical components of an instruction. We adopt a targeted injection strategy:

text[[85, 141, 480, 196]]
A.7.1 Key Entity Extraction. We employ a hybrid NER framework (FLERT + GPT-4o) for robust entity identification. After filtering, we obtained 31,140 entities across four high-risk categories: ID, Date/Time, Email, and URL.

text[[85, 204, 480, 245]]
A.7.2 Malicious Unicode Injection. We applied differentiated injection strategies based on entity type (detailed in ), ultimately yielding 5,089 malicious instruction samples.

text[[85, 252, 480, 333]]
A.7.3 Synthesis of Benign Unicode Data (Negative Samples). To enable discrimination between malicious tampering and benign usage, we generated negative samples by injecting Unicode characters exclusively into non-critical regions. This trains the model to tolerate benign noise without compromising sensitive entity integrity.

sub_title[[86, 386, 365, 402]]
## A.8 SFT for Binary Classification

text[[85, 405, 480, 544]]
To instantiate the Semantic Verification Layer of GSIGuard, we fine- tuned the Qwen3- 4B base model on our synthesized corpus. We formulated the training objective as a binary classification task via supervised fine- tuning (SFT). Unlike standard open- ended generation tasks, we constrained the model's output space to the set {Benign, Malicious}. Each input sequence is encapsulated within a structured prompt template designed to direct the model's attention mechanism toward potential Unicode anomalies: "Analyze the following instruction for hidden Unicode injections: [I]".
